title: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis
authors: Jordan B. Jastrab, Marcia B. Goldberg

items.subtopic: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis | ENTRY INTO THE HUMAN HOST
items.title: Mechanisms and Routes of Microbial Entry into the Human Host
items.content: Understanding how pathogens enter the human body is crucial for preventing and managing infections. Pathogens can cross tissue barriers or penetrate skin/mucous membranes, leading to localized or systemic infections.
keyPoints: Pathogens may enter through respiratory droplet nuclei (1–5 μm) or fomites.
Coughs generate ~3000 particles; sneezes can produce up to 40,000 particles.
Large particles may reduce in size to 0.5 μm as they travel through the respiratory tract.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis | ENTRY INTO THE HUMAN HOST

items.subtopic: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis | ENTRY INTO THE HUMAN HOST
items.title: Pathogen Entry into the Gastrointestinal Tract and Infectious Inoculum Size
items.content: The route of entry for gastrointestinal pathogens is often through ingestion, with varying infectious inoculum sizes dictating transmission modes.
keyPoints: Contaminated food or water typically transmits pathogens requiring large inocula (e.g., $10^{8}$ organisms for Vibrio cholerae).
Person-to-person spread occurs with pathogens needing smaller inocula (e.g., $10^{1} - 10^{2}$ organisms for Shigella spp.).
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis | ENTRY INTO THE HUMAN HOST

items.subtopic: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis | ENTRY INTO THE HUMAN HOST
items.title: Entry into the Genitourinary Tract and Pyelonephritis Risk
items.content: Pathogens can enter the genitourinary tract through fecal contamination or medical instrumentation, leading to infections like pyelonephritis.
keyPoints: Pyelonephritis may result from hematogenous seeding of the kidney.
Colonization of the urethral meatus or vaginal introitus with fecal organisms can lead to ascension into the bladder or kidneys.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis | ENTRY INTO THE HUMAN HOST

items.subtopic: NICHE
items.title: Pathogen Tropism and Niche Establishment
items.content: Bacterial pathogens exhibit tissue tropism, meaning they infect specific sites within the host. This specificity is influenced by how pathogens access their niche and interact with host factors such as glycan-decorated receptors and the extracellular matrix. Environmental conditions in these niches can trigger virulence factor expression essential for infection.
keyPoints: Pathogen-specific infections: Group A streptococci cause pharyngitis, not pneumonia; Cholera affects the small intestine; Shigella spp. target the rectosigmoid colon.
Mode of spread determines pathogen settlement in respiratory tree (e.g., droplet nuclei reach bronchial tree or alveoli).
Virulence factors are triggered by environmental conditions: Bile salts stimulate V. cholerae adhesins and C. difficile spore germination.
Pathogens manipulate their environment to facilitate infection, e.g., H. pylori increases stomach pH via urea conversion.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · NICHE

items.subtopic: NICHE
items.title: Diseases Caused by Preformed Toxins
items.content: Certain diseases result from ingestion of preformed bacterial toxins rather than active infection. These include S. aureus enterotoxins and B. cereus emetic toxin, which are heat-stable and can cause severe symptoms like vomiting. Botulinum toxin, although produced by C. botulinum, is notable for causing flaccid paralysis due to its action on neurotransmitter release.
keyPoints: Preformed toxins: S. aureus enterotoxins in dairy/meat; B. cereus emetic toxin in improperly refrigerated rice.
Toxins are heat-stable, unlike the bacteria that produce them.
Botulinum toxin causes flaccid paralysis by blocking neurotransmitter release and can lead to fatal respiratory failure.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · NICHE

items.subtopic: ATTACHMENT (part 1)
items.title: Role of Adhesins in Bacterial Pathogenesis
items.content: Adhesins are surface structures that enable bacteria to attach to host tissues, facilitating infection establishment and evasion from immune responses. They include pili, flagella, and autotransporter proteins.
keyPoints: Adhesion is mediated by receptor-ligand interactions specific to host cell surfaces.
Pili (type I, type P, type IV) are crucial for mucosal surface adherence; e.g., UPEC uses type I pili for bladder infections.
Flagella aid in motility and can act as adhesins, important for pathogens like EPEC and EHEC.
Autotransporter proteins function as virulence determinants, essential for intimate host-pathogen interactions.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · ATTACHMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: ATTACHMENT (part 1)
items.title: Mechanisms of Adhesion and Immune Evasion
items.content: Bacterial adhesins facilitate attachment to host tissues, contributing to biofilm formation and immune evasion. Therapeutic blockade may exacerbate infections due to triggered signaling.
keyPoints: Adhesins enable bacteria to avoid clearance by the innate immune system.
Biofilms formed through adhesion protect pathogens from immune responses.
Blocking adhesion can trigger cellular signal transduction, potentially worsening infection.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · ATTACHMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: ATTACHMENT (part 1)
items.title: Diversity and Function of Bacterial Adhesins
items.content: Bacteria produce multiple adhesins with varying specificities to interact with different host receptors. These interactions are often redundant and serologically variable.
keyPoints: Adhesins include pili, flagella, autotransporter proteins, Opa family proteins, and MSCRAMMs.
Pili have varied functions: type I for mucosal adherence, type IV for DNA uptake and motility.
Flagella are related to T3SSs and can secrete toxins or bind to host surfaces.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · ATTACHMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: ATTACHMENT (part 2)
items.title: Role of Glycans in Bacterial Adherence and Susceptibility
items.content: Glycans on human cells are crucial for bacterial adherence. Individuals lacking functional FUT2 enzyme exhibit altered susceptibility to infections, highlighting the importance of glycans.
keyPoints: 20% of humans have nonfunctional FUT2 alleles.
Lack of functional FUT2 increases susceptibility to certain bacterial infections and decreases susceptibility to some viral infections.
Increased secretion of sialylated glycans in individuals lacking functional FUT2.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · ATTACHMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: ATTACHMENT (part 2)
items.title: GM1 Ganglioside as a Receptor for Bacterial Toxins
items.content: GM1 ganglioside serves as a receptor for cholera toxin, Pseudomonas aeruginosa flagella, and Clostridium perfringens alpha toxin, influencing bacterial tropism.
keyPoints: Receptor for cholera toxin (CTX).
Receptor for Pseudomonas aeruginosa flagella.
Receptor for Clostridium perfringens alpha toxin.
Contributes to H. pylori tropism via BabA and SabA interactions.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · ATTACHMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: ATTACHMENT (part 2)
items.title: CFTR's Role in Bacterial Adherence and Cystic Fibrosis Susceptibility
items.content: CFTR, a chloride channel, is involved in mucosal hydration and serves as a receptor for bacterial adherence, impacting cystic fibrosis patients' susceptibility to infections.
keyPoints: Mutation of CFTR gene causes cystic fibrosis.
Cystic fibrosis patients are hypersusceptible to respiratory infections due to impaired mucus clearance.
High frequency of cystic fibrosis in Caucasians (~2.5%) linked to resistance to Salmonella Typhi infection.
CFTR is a receptor for Pseudomonas aeruginosa, affecting pathogen clearance.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · ATTACHMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: REPLICATIVE NICHE (part 1)
items.title: Mechanisms of Avoiding Phagocytosis by Extracellular Pathogens
items.content: Extracellular pathogens employ various strategies to avoid or inhibit phagocytosis, allowing them to persist and replicate at the host cell surface. These mechanisms include secretion of cytoskeleton-disrupting proteins, formation of biofilms, and delivery of bacterial proteins that inhibit phagocytic processes.
keyPoints: Extracellular pathogens like Pseudomonas aeruginosa form biofilms which protect against phagocytosis.
Vibrio cholerae secretes mucinase vibriolysin to degrade mucus barriers and inhibits further phagocytosis of extracellular bacteria.
Yersinia spp. deliver proteins into leukocytes that inhibit phagocytosis, allowing surface association.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · REPLICATIVE NICHE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REPLICATIVE NICHE (part 1)
items.title: Intracellular Survival Mechanisms in Phagocytes
items.content: Certain pathogens have evolved to survive within phagocytes by resisting the destructive processes of these immune cells. This allows them to persist and replicate within host tissues.
keyPoints: Streptococcus pneumoniae survives within vacuoles in dendritic cells and macrophages during colonization of the nasopharynx.
Pathogens like Salmonella spp. and Listeria monocytogenes induce internalization into normally phagocytic cells such as macrophages.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · REPLICATIVE NICHE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REPLICATIVE NICHE (part 1)
items.title: Type III Secretion System in Bacterial Internalization
items.content: The type III secretion system (T3SS) is a critical mechanism used by certain bacterial pathogens to deliver effector proteins into host cells, facilitating their internalization and altering cellular processes to promote infection.
keyPoints: T3SS resembles a needle and syringe apparatus that delivers bacterial proteins directly into the cytosol of eukaryotic cells.
Salmonella, Shigella, and Chlamydia spp. use T3SS to induce entry by activating actin polymerization, forming membrane ruffles that engulf the bacterium.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · REPLICATIVE NICHE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REPLICATIVE NICHE (part 2)
items.title: Zipper Mechanism of Bacterial Entry
items.content: Certain invasive pathogens enter host cells via a zipper-like mechanism that does not require the Type III Secretion System (T3SS). This process involves bacterial surface molecules binding to and clustering host cell receptors, leading to membrane wrapping around the bacterium. Examples include Yersinia pseudotuberculosis engaging β1-integrins and Listeria monocytogenes interacting with E-cadherin and Met receptor.
keyPoints: Zipper mechanism does not require T3SS.
Y. pseudotuberculosis binds β1-integrins, activating PI3K/Akt signaling for entry.
L. monocytogenes engages E-cadherin and Met receptor, activating β-catenin signaling.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · REPLICATIVE NICHE (part 2)

items.subtopic: REPLICATIVE NICHE (part 2)
items.title: Cytosolic Bacteria Entry and Spread
items.content: Certain bacteria initially enter host cells into vacuoles or phagosomes but escape to the cytosol. Listeria monocytogenes uses listeriolysin O and phospholipases for this escape, while Shigella spp.'s mechanism is less defined but requires T3SS. Once in the cytosol, these bacteria utilize actin-based motility to spread between cells, with some pathogens like Burkholderia pseudomallei using a Type VI Secretion System (T6SS) to induce cell fusion.
keyPoints: L. monocytogenes escapes vacuoles via listeriolysin O and phospholipases.
Shigella spp. requires T3SS for vacuolar escape.
Actin-based motility is used by L. monocytogenes, Shigella spp., B. pseudomallei, and Rickettsia spp. to spread between cells.
Burkholderia spp. induce cell fusion via T6SS.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · REPLICATIVE NICHE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SURVIVAL IN THE VACUOLE | TYPE III SECRETION SYSTEMS | TYPE IV SECRETION SYSTEMS
items.title: Mechanisms of Intracellular Survival by Bacterial Pathogens
items.content: Invasive bacterial pathogens often survive within host cells by manipulating vacuolar processes. They can block endosomal maturation and lysosomal degradation, or escape into the cytosol to replicate. Type III secretion systems (T3SS) enable bacteria like S. enterica serovar Typhimurium, Chlamydia spp., and Shigella spp. to either remain in vacuoles or escape them by delivering effector proteins that manipulate host processes.
keyPoints: Bacterial pathogens subvert endosomal trafficking to avoid degradation.
T3SSs are used by bacteria like P. aeruginosa, S. enterica serovar Typhimurium, Chlamydia spp., and Shigella spp.
S. enterica uses T3SS for both invasion and maintenance within the SCV.
Shigella escapes vacuoles using T3SS, while Chlamydia remains in the inclusion.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · SURVIVAL IN THE VACUOLE | TYPE III SECRETION SYSTEMS | TYPE IV SECRETION SYSTEMS

items.subtopic: SURVIVAL IN THE VACUOLE | TYPE III SECRETION SYSTEMS | TYPE IV SECRETION SYSTEMS
items.title: Role of Type IV Secretion Systems (T4SS) in Bacterial Pathogenesis
items.content: Type IV secretion systems are crucial for the survival of certain pathogens within phagocytic vacuoles. They deliver effector proteins that alter vacuole maturation, preventing lysosomal fusion and manipulating host vesicular trafficking. T4SSs are structurally diverse and functionally more versatile than T3SSs, being present in both gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria.
keyPoints: T4SSs deliver effector proteins into the eukaryotic cell cytosol.
They prevent lysosomal fusion with vacuoles, aiding bacterial survival.
L. pneumophila and Coxiella burnetii use T4SS to create LCV and CCV respectively.
T4SSs are more structurally diverse than T3SSs.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · SURVIVAL IN THE VACUOLE | TYPE III SECRETION SYSTEMS | TYPE IV SECRETION SYSTEMS

items.subtopic: SURVIVAL IN THE VACUOLE | TYPE III SECRETION SYSTEMS | TYPE IV SECRETION SYSTEMS
items.title: Distinct Characteristics of Legionella-containing and Coxiella-containing Vacuoles
items.content: Legionella pneumophila and Coxiella burnetii create specialized vacuoles (LCV and CCV) that facilitate their survival. LCVs resemble the endoplasmic reticulum, while CCVs have lysosome-like features with a low pH. Effector proteins delivered by T4SS modify these vacuoles to prevent degradation and promote bacterial replication.
keyPoints: LCVs resemble cellular endoplasmic reticulum due to effector protein action.
CCVs are lysosome-like, with low pH activating further effector delivery.
Effector proteins in LCVs block Rab5 GTPase recruitment, preventing maturation.
In CCVs, bacterial effectors increase membrane size by recruiting vesicles.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · SURVIVAL IN THE VACUOLE | TYPE III SECRETION SYSTEMS | TYPE IV SECRETION SYSTEMS

items.subtopic: OTHER SECRETION SYSTEMS | NUTRIENT ACQUISITION | CELLULAR TRAFFICKING
items.title: Mechanisms of Phagosomal Escape and Nutrient Acquisition by Intracellular Pathogens
items.content: Intracellular pathogens like Francisella tularensis and Mycobacterium tuberculosis utilize secretion systems to manipulate host cells for survival. F. tularensis delays phagosome maturation using its type VI secretion system (T6SS) and escapes into the cytosol to replicate, while M. tuberculosis uses the ESX-1 type VII secretion system (T7SS) for phagosomal membrane lysis.
keyPoints: F. tularensis delays phagosome maturation using T6SS.
M. tuberculosis requires ESX-1 T7SS for phagosomal membrane lysis.
Few effectors of these secretion systems are characterized, and the process of phagosomal membrane lysis is poorly understood.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · OTHER SECRETION SYSTEMS | NUTRIENT ACQUISITION | CELLULAR TRAFFICKING

items.subtopic: OTHER SECRETION SYSTEMS | NUTRIENT ACQUISITION | CELLULAR TRAFFICKING
items.title: Bacterial Strategies for Nutrient Acquisition and Cellular Trafficking Manipulation
items.content: Pathogens employ various strategies to acquire nutrients and manipulate host cellular trafficking. They secrete metal ion-binding proteins under oxidative stress, upregulate cell surface receptors, and deliver factors that inhibit host mRNA translation. Vacuolar pathogens modulate endosomal trafficking using effector proteins mimicking host GTPases and SNAREs, redirecting host vesicles to deliver nutrients.
keyPoints: Pathogens secrete metal ion-binding proteins via T4SS under oxidative stress.
Effector proteins mimic host GTPases (GEFs) and SNAREs for trafficking manipulation.
L. pneumophila uses its T4SS to deliver >300 effector proteins, including AnkX and SidM, which modify Rab1.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · OTHER SECRETION SYSTEMS | NUTRIENT ACQUISITION | CELLULAR TRAFFICKING

items.subtopic: AVOIDANCE OF INNATE IMMUNE RESPONSES | COMPLEMENT
items.title: Pathogen Evasion of Complement System
items.content: Bacteria have evolved mechanisms to evade the complement system, a crucial component of innate immunity that aids in pathogen clearance through opsonization and lysis.
keyPoints: Complement activation can occur via classical, lectin, or alternative pathways.
Strategies for evasion include capsule formation, LPS modification, degradation of complement proteins, and surface masking with host proteins.
Streptococci use proteases to degrade complement proteins and M proteins to bind C4BP, preventing C3b deposition.
Borrelia burgdorferi inhibits membrane attack complex assembly via a CD59-like protein.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · AVOIDANCE OF INNATE IMMUNE RESPONSES | COMPLEMENT

items.subtopic: AVOIDANCE OF INNATE IMMUNE RESPONSES | COMPLEMENT
items.title: Role of M Cells in Pathogen Entry and Immune Activation
items.content: M cells facilitate pathogen entry into the gut-associated lymphoid tissue, initiating immune responses by delivering antigens to dendritic cells, macrophages, and neutrophils.
keyPoints: M cells sample antigens from the gut lumen through transcytosis.
Dendritic cells, macrophages, and neutrophils in gut-associated lymphoid tissue process these antigens for MHC presentation.
Antigen-presenting cells migrate to lymph nodes to stimulate adaptive immunity.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · AVOIDANCE OF INNATE IMMUNE RESPONSES | COMPLEMENT

items.subtopic: AVOIDANCE OF INNATE IMMUNE RESPONSES | COMPLEMENT
items.title: Bacterial Strategies to Promote Phagocytosis and Avoid Destruction
items.content: Certain bacteria exploit phagocytic cells for replication by promoting uptake while preventing lysosomal fusion, thus evading destruction.
keyPoints: Francisella and Yersinia spp. promote opsonization to enhance phagocytosis.
These bacteria replicate within the phagosomal compartment after entry.
They prevent lysosomal fusion, avoiding degradation by host cells.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · AVOIDANCE OF INNATE IMMUNE RESPONSES | COMPLEMENT

items.subtopic: LYSOSOMES | ANTIMICROBIAL PEPTIDES | OXIDATIVE BURST
items.title: Role of Lysosomal Enzymes in Antimicrobial Defense
items.content: Lysosomes contain hydrolytic and antimicrobial enzymes that are crucial for recycling cellular material and killing microbes. Lysozyme, a lysosomal enzyme, targets the peptidoglycan layer of gram-positive bacteria, leading to bacterial lysis. However, it is less effective against gram-negative bacteria due to their protective outer membrane.
keyPoints: Lysozyme hydrolyzes polymeric glycan chains in peptidoglycan, causing bacterial lysis.
Effective primarily against gram-positive bacteria; gram-negative bacteria are protected by an outer membrane.
Gram-positive bacteria can modify peptidoglycan to resist lysozyme action (e.g., acetylation of sugars).
Mitochondria-derived metabolite itaconate accumulates in lysosomes during infection, limiting bacterial growth.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · LYSOSOMES | ANTIMICROBIAL PEPTIDES | OXIDATIVE BURST

items.subtopic: LYSOSOMES | ANTIMICROBIAL PEPTIDES | OXIDATIVE BURST
items.title: Mechanisms of Oxidative Burst in Immune Defense
items.content: The oxidative burst involves the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by NADPH oxidase, which is crucial for killing pathogens. Neutrophils use this mechanism to generate superoxide radicals that destroy bacteria within phagosomes or extracellularly.
keyPoints: NADPH oxidase produces superoxide radicals via electron transfer to molecular oxygen.
Reactive oxygen species are generated in the phagosome of neutrophils and/or extracellular space.
Bacteria have detoxification enzymes (e.g., superoxide dismutases, peroxiredoxins, catalases) to survive oxidative burst.
Some pathogens inhibit NADPH oxidase assembly via T3SS effector proteins targeting small GTPases.
Chronic granulomatous disease patients are highly susceptible to bacterial and fungal infections due to non-functional NADPH oxidase.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · LYSOSOMES | ANTIMICROBIAL PEPTIDES | OXIDATIVE BURST

items.subtopic: NEUTROPHIL EXTRACELLULAR TRAPS | PATTERN RECOGNITION RECEPTORS, GUARD PROTEINS, AND THEIR EVASION
items.title: Role of NETs in Pathogen Evasion by S. aureus
items.content: Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) are released during oxidative bursts to trap and destroy pathogens. However, Streptococcus species and Staphylococcus aureus can evade this mechanism. Streptococci produce DNases that degrade NETs, while S. aureus nuclease breaks down NETs into nucleotide products, which it then converts into deoxyadenosine—a macrophage toxin.
keyPoints: NETs are composed of decondensed chromatin designed to ensnare pathogens.
Streptococci and S. aureus can degrade NETs using DNases and nucleases, respectively.
S. aureus converts degraded nucleotide products into deoxyadenosine, which acts as a macrophage toxin.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · NEUTROPHIL EXTRACELLULAR TRAPS | PATTERN RECOGNITION RECEPTORS, GUARD PROTEINS, AND THEIR EVASION

items.subtopic: NEUTROPHIL EXTRACELLULAR TRAPS | PATTERN RECOGNITION RECEPTORS, GUARD PROTEINS, AND THEIR EVASION
items.title: Mechanism of Pathogen Detection by Pattern Recognition Receptors (PRRs)
items.content: The innate immune system detects pathogens through pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) that identify pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs). PRRs are divided into membrane-associated and cytosolic types, with the latter eliciting stronger proinflammatory responses. Bacteri-derived PAMPs typically provoke a more intense inflammatory response than virus-derived ones.
keyPoints: PRRs detect pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) to signal infection.
Cytosolic PRRs trigger stronger proinflammatory responses compared to membrane-associated PRRs.
Bacteri-derived PAMPs elicit more powerful inflammatory responses than virus-derived PAMPs.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · NEUTROPHIL EXTRACELLULAR TRAPS | PATTERN RECOGNITION RECEPTORS, GUARD PROTEINS, AND THEIR EVASION

items.subtopic: TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 1)
items.title: TLR4 Recognition and Pathogen Evasion
items.content: TLR4 efficiently binds to hexa-acylated lipid A, a component of LPS. However, pathogens like Y. pestis can evade immune detection by switching to tetra-acylated LPS upon entering the human host, which does not activate TLR4.
keyPoints: TLR4 recognizes hexa-acylated lipid A in LPS.
Y. pestis switches from hexa-acylated to tetra-acylated LPS to evade immune detection.
Tetra-acylated LPS is also produced by F. tularensis, H. pylori, and Porphyromonas gingivalis.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 1)
items.title: Inhibition of NF-κB Signaling by Bacterial Effectors
items.content: Bacteria can inhibit NF-κB signaling, a critical pathway for antibacterial and steady-state immunity, through various mechanisms. These include the use of E3 ubiquitin ligases to degrade components of the NF-κB pathway or bacterial deubiquitinases that prevent degradation of IκBα.
keyPoints: NF-κB signaling is crucial for controlling antibacterial and steady-state immunity.
Shigella spp. use T3SS to deliver E3 ubiquitin ligases targeting NF-κB pathway components.
C. trachomatis and B. pseudomallei secrete deubiquitinases that prevent IκBα degradation.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 1)
items.title: Inhibition of MAPK Signaling by Bacterial Effectors
items.content: Bacteria can inhibit the MAPK phosphorylation cascade, which activates AP-1 transcription factor. This is achieved through various mechanisms such as cleaving MAPKs or inhibiting their phosphorylation.
keyPoints: MAPK signaling activates AP-1 and is crucial for pro-inflammatory responses.
B. anthracis lethal factor cleaves the N-terminal region of MAPKs, disrupting the kinase cascade.
Shigella spp. and S. enterica serovar Typhimurium produce phosphothreonine lyases that prevent phosphorylation of MAPKs.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 1)

items.subtopic: TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 2)
items.title: Role of NOD1 and NOD2 in Inflammatory Bowel Disease
items.content: NOD1 and NOD2 are cytosolic pattern recognition receptors that detect modified peptidoglycan fragments, activating NF-κB signaling. Polymorphisms in these genes can predispose individuals to inflammatory bowel disease.
keyPoints: NOD1 and NOD2 activate NF-κB upon binding modified peptidoglycan fragments.
Polymorphisms in NOD1 and NOD2 are linked to susceptibility to inflammatory bowel disease.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 2)

items.subtopic: TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 2)
items.title: cGAS-STING Pathway in Cytosolic DNA Recognition
items.content: The cGAS-STING pathway is activated by cytosolic DNA, leading to the synthesis of cGAMP and subsequent activation of IRF3 and NF-κB. This promotes transcription of interferon-regulated proinflammatory genes.
keyPoints: cGAS synthesizes cGAMP in response to cytosolic DNA.
STING activates IRF3 and NF-κB, promoting transcription of proinflammatory genes.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 2)

items.subtopic: TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 2)
items.title: Inflammasome Activation and Pyroptosis
items.content: Inflammasomes, such as those formed by NAIP-NLRC4 and AIM2, activate caspases that cleave GSDMD and IL-1 family cytokines. This leads to pyroptosis and robust inflammation.
keyPoints: Inflammasomes activate caspases like caspase-1.
Caspase activation results in cleavage of GSDMD and IL-1β, leading to pyroptosis.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 2)

items.subtopic: TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 3)
items.title: Role of Caspases-4 and -5 in Noncanonical Inflammasome Activation
items.content: Caspases-4 and -5 are pivotal in the noncanonical activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome by LPS. They cleave GSDMD, leading to membrane pore formation that indirectly activates the NLRP3 inflammasome, which then cleaves IL-1β.
keyPoints: Caspases-4 and -5 can act as PRRs for LPS in human cells.
Activation of caspases leads to GSDMD cleavage but not IL-1β directly.
GSDMD membrane pores facilitate NLRP3 inflammasome activation, resulting in IL-1β cleavage.
Caspase-11 homolog in mice drives mortality in sepsis models.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 3)

items.subtopic: TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 3)
items.title: Immune GTPases and Pathogen Interaction
items.content: GBPs are immune GTPases that respond to pathogen detection by promoting PAMP release from pathogens, aiding in inflammasome activation. They also play roles in lysing certain bacteria and preventing bacterial motility.
keyPoints: GBPs are recruited to pathogen-containing vacuoles or membranes.
GBPs facilitate noncanonical inflammasome activation by extracting LPS.
Contribute to lysis of F. novicida and Neisseria meningitidis, releasing bacterial DNA.
Shigella spp. secrete effectors that ubiquitinate GBPs for degradation.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 3)

items.subtopic: TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 3)
items.title: Bacterial Strategies to Inhibit Host Cell Death
items.content: Pathogens like S. enterica and Yersinia employ mechanisms to inhibit apoptosis, which can lead to alternative cell death pathways such as necroptosis. This highlights the redundancy in host cell death pathways.
keyPoints: S. enterica and Yersinia modify or cleave components of death receptor-associated centers to block apoptosis.
Inhibition of extrinsic apoptotic signaling may promote necroptosis.
Cyclomodulins like Cif disrupt epithelial turnover by inactivating E3 ligases.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 3)

items.subtopic: TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 4)
items.title: Mechanisms by which intracellular pathogens inhibit autophagy
items.content: Intracellular bacterial pathogens employ various strategies to inhibit autophagy, crucial for their survival. These mechanisms include masking ubiquitination sites on the bacterial surface, inhibiting autophagosome maturation, and altering host pathways that regulate autophagy.
keyPoints: L. monocytogenes recruits Arp2/3 to mask its surface, preventing recognition by E3 ligases.
F. tularensis uses an LPS O-antigen to block access of E3 ligases.
S. flexneri produces an effector protein that inhibits ubiquitination of IcsA.
L. pneumophila secretes an effector that cleaves LC3, disrupting autophagosomal flux.
S. enterica serovar Typhimurium blocks kinase activation necessary for autophagosome generation.
M. tuberculosis and C. trachomatis prevent autophagosomelysosome fusion.
Intracellular pathogens can reactivate mTOR signaling to blunt autophagy.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 4)

items.subtopic: TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 4)
items.title: Epigenetic modulation of host immune responses by pathogens
items.content: Pathogens can manipulate host immune responses through epigenetic mechanisms, influencing chromatin structure and gene expression. This includes the delivery of histone methyltransferases that control inflammation and ribosome activity.
keyPoints: Innate immune training involves epigenetic priming via chromatin alterations for appropriate immune response amplitude.
Microbiota can modify methylation states of immunity-related gene promoters, tuning infection responses.
B. anthracis and L. pneumophila deliver histone methyltransferases to control inflammation and ribosome activity.
L. monocytogenes listeriolysin O and P. aeruginosa T3SS pore alter ion homeostasis for epigenetic modulation of gene expression.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR DETECTION OF EXTRACELLULAR AND PHAGOSOMAL LIGANDS (part 4)

items.subtopic: INHIBITION OF ADAPTIVE IMMUNE RESPONSES
items.title: Mechanisms by which M. tuberculosis evades adaptive immunity
items.content: M. tuberculosis establishes latent infections by inducing immunosuppressive cytokines such as IL-6, IL-10, and transforming growth factor β. This results in the inhibition of interferon-γ-dependent gene expression, leading to downregulation of MHC class II molecules, which hampers the activation of helper CD4+ T lymphocytes.
keyPoints: M. tuberculosis induces secretion of IL-6, IL-10, and transforming growth factor β.
Inhibition of interferon-γ-dependent gene expression occurs.
Downregulation of MHC class II molecules inhibits induction of helper CD4+ T lymphocytes.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · INHIBITION OF ADAPTIVE IMMUNE RESPONSES

items.subtopic: INHIBITION OF ADAPTIVE IMMUNE RESPONSES
items.title: Pathogen strategies to directly alter B-cell and T-cell activity
items.content: Yersinia uses the T3SS effector YopH, a protein tyrosine phosphatase, to dephosphorylate B-cell and T-cell receptors, preventing signal transduction and lymphocyte activation. S. aureus produces superantigens that bind invariant regions of MHC molecules and T-cell receptors, leading to nonspecific activation of vast numbers of T cells and a cytokine storm.
keyPoints: YopH dephosphorylates B-cell and T-cell receptors, preventing signal transduction.
S. aureus superantigens bind invariant regions of MHC molecules and T-cell receptors.
Nonspecific activation of vast numbers of T cells leads to a cytokine storm.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · INHIBITION OF ADAPTIVE IMMUNE RESPONSES

items.subtopic: BACTERIAL CYTOTOXINS
items.title: AB Toxins and Their Mechanisms in Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: AB toxins, such as Shiga toxin and diphtheria toxin, consist of an A subunit with enzymatic activity and a B subunit that binds to cellular receptors. Upon endocytosis, the A subunit translocates into the cytosol, where it exerts its toxic effect by modifying host cell functions.
keyPoints: AB toxins include Shiga toxin (Shigella spp., E. coli), diphtheria toxin (Corynebacterium diphtheriae), pertussis toxin (B. pertussis), and CTX (V. cholerae).
The A subunit's enzymatic activity is stimulated in the cytosol after endocytosis.
CTX and pertussis toxin ADP-ribosylate G-protein regulators of adenylate cyclases, increasing cAMP concentrations.
Increased cAMP disrupts ion homeostasis and apoptosis; on a tissue level, it induces chloride secretion via CFTR and inhibits sodium chloride absorption, causing diarrhea in cholera.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · BACTERIAL CYTOTOXINS

items.subtopic: BACTERIAL CYTOTOXINS
items.title: Pore-Forming Toxins and Their Role in Immune Evasion
items.content: Pore-forming toxins, such as those produced by S. aureus (leukocidins, hemolysins) and other pathogens like S. pneumoniae, disrupt host cell membranes leading to lysis. These toxins help bacteria evade the immune system by preventing phagocytosis and adaptive immune responses.
keyPoints: Pore-forming toxins are produced by extracellular pathogens including S. aureus (leukocidins, hemolysins), C. perfringens, B. anthracis, L. monocytogenes, and S. pneumoniae.
Leukocidins and $\alpha$- and $\gamma$-hemolysins oligomerize within host plasma membranes causing cell lysis.
Phenol-soluble modulins are small peptides that insert into cell membranes directly.
Lytic toxins protect S. aureus from phagocytosis and hinder the development of a protective adaptive immune response.
Host cells can counteract cytolysins by releasing exosomes, which act as decoys to remove these toxins.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · BACTERIAL CYTOTOXINS

items.subtopic: TISSUE DAMAGE AND PATHOGEN DISSEMINATION
items.title: Mechanisms of Intestinal Pathogen Dissemination
items.content: Intestinal pathogens disrupt gut epithelium integrity by manipulating cell polarity or disrupting intercellular junctions, facilitating deeper tissue penetration and systemic spread.
keyPoints: C. perfringens, V. cholerae, pathogenic E. coli, Shigella spp., S. enterica serovar Typhi disrupt tight junctions in the gastrointestinal tract.
V. cholerae's RtxA toxin causes cell rounding and barrier failure through actin-crosslinking while avoiding immune response by inactivating phospholipases and Rho GTPases.
Shigella spp. and some pathogenic E. coli secrete SPATEs that cleave components of epithelial junctions and mucins, aiding tissue penetration.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TISSUE DAMAGE AND PATHOGEN DISSEMINATION

items.subtopic: TISSUE DAMAGE AND PATHOGEN DISSEMINATION
items.title: Transmission Dynamics of Bacterial Pathogens
items.content: Bacterial pathogens exploit host damage to facilitate transmission to new hosts through mechanisms aligned with their mode of entry, such as aerosolization in respiratory infections and fecal-oral routes in gastrointestinal infections.
keyPoints: Respiratory pathogens are transmitted via coughing-induced aerosolization following lung tissue damage.
Gastrointestinal pathogens cause diarrhea, facilitating transmission via the fecal–oral route or contamination of food and crops.
Hygiene practices like hand washing and decontamination reduce transmission rates.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · TISSUE DAMAGE AND PATHOGEN DISSEMINATION

items.subtopic: INHIBITION OF ADAPTIVE IMMUNE RESPONSES
items.assertion: B. pertussis induces dendritic cells to produce IL-10.
items.reason: This skews T-cell maturation into regulatory T cells, dampening the immune response.
items.explanation: The production of IL-10 by dendritic cells induced by B. pertussis leads to a shift towards regulatory T-cell development, which suppresses the immune response.
items.reference: Molecular Mechanisms of Microbial Pathogenesis · INHIBITION OF ADAPTIVE IMMUNE RESPONSES
